title: Framework Comparison Matrix
description: Side-by-side comparison of 6 major AI governance frameworks across 10 key governance dimensions. Color-coded by strength of coverage: strong (comprehensive/binding requirements), partial (addressed but limited), or not addressed.

eu-ai-act.summary: All AI systems placed on EU market. Extraterritorial — applies to non-EU providers serving EU users. Covers providers, deployers, importers, distributors.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: All AI stakeholders in Malaysia — developers, providers, users. Voluntary adoption across all sectors. No extraterritorial reach.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Technology-neutral, applicable to any AI system by any organization. Voluntary — no mandated scope. Referenced in US federal procurement.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: All AI actors across the lifecycle in 46 adopting countries. Broad scope but non-binding — influences national legislation.

singapore-maigf.summary: Organizations deploying AI commercially in Singapore. Sector-agnostic. Agentic AI addendum (2025) extends to autonomous agents.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: All 193 member states' AI policies. Broadest geographic scope of any framework but non-binding.

eu-ai-act.summary: 4-tier system: prohibited, high-risk (Annex III), limited risk, minimal risk. Plus GPAI with systemic risk tier. Most prescriptive classification globally.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: No risk classification tiers. Principles-based approach without differentiating AI systems by risk level.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Context-dependent risk assessment through MAP and MEASURE functions. No fixed tiers — organizations define risk criteria per deployment context.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: No formal risk classification. Recommends risk-proportionate governance but does not define categories.

singapore-maigf.summary: Risk-proportionate approach recommended but no fixed tiers. Organizations self-assess based on probability and severity of harm.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: No risk classification. Values-based rather than risk-based approach to AI governance.

eu-ai-act.summary: Comprehensive: Art. 13 instructions for use, Art. 50 deepfake/AI content disclosure, Art. 85 right to explanation for high-risk decisions. Strongest transparency regime.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 5 (Transparency): disclose AI use, provide explanations of decisions, maintain audit trails. Principle-level without specific mechanisms.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: MAP-1.5, GOVERN-1.5, MEASURE-2.8/2.9 address explainability. Detailed operational guidance for transparency implementation.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 3: meaningful transparency and responsible disclosure. Foundational — influenced most national frameworks.

singapore-maigf.summary: Operations Management principle includes explainability. Practical guidance on explaining AI decisions to affected individuals.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Principle 11: transparency and explainability as core values. Policy action area on transparency implementation.

eu-ai-act.summary: Clear provider/deployer obligations with designated responsibilities. Conformity assessment for high-risk. AI Office for GPAI oversight. Legal persons identified.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 6 (Accountability): organizations should be answerable for AI outcomes. No designated responsible officers or legal obligations.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: GOVERN function establishes organizational accountability structures. Roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths defined. No legal enforcement.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 5: AI actors should be accountable. Recommends mechanisms but leaves implementation to national frameworks.

singapore-maigf.summary: Internal governance structures recommended. Board/C-suite accountability for AI governance. Practical organizational models provided.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Accountability as a core value. Recommends liability frameworks and redress mechanisms. Non-binding.

eu-ai-act.summary: Complements GDPR. Art. 10 data governance for training data. Biometric data restrictions. Data protection impact assessments for high-risk AI.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 4 (Privacy and Security): protect personal data in AI systems. References PDPA (Act 709) but no AI-specific data requirements.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: MAP and MEASURE address data privacy risks. References NIST Privacy Framework. No binding data protection requirements.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: References privacy as a value. Defers to national data protection laws for specific requirements.

singapore-maigf.summary: References PDPA compliance. Data management guidance for AI training data. AI Verify includes data governance testing.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Data protection as a core principle. Policy action area on data governance. Broad guidance without specific mechanisms.

eu-ai-act.summary: Art. 10 bias monitoring in training data. Prohibited uses include social scoring and emotion recognition in certain contexts. Conformity assessment covers bias.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 3 (Fairness): prevent discrimination, ensure equitable outcomes. Principle 2 (Inclusiveness): diverse stakeholder representation.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: MAP-2.3, MEASURE-2.6/2.10/2.11 address bias detection and mitigation. Companion NIST AI 100-4 provides bias management guidance.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 1: inclusive growth and sustainable development. Fairness embedded in values but no specific bias testing requirements.

singapore-maigf.summary: Fairness principle with practical guidance. AI Verify testing toolkit includes fairness metrics. Sector-specific fairness benchmarks.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Fairness and non-discrimination as core values. Strongest emphasis on equity among international frameworks.

eu-ai-act.summary: Art. 14: mandatory human oversight for high-risk AI. Deployers must enable human intervention. Stop mechanisms required. Strongest human-in-the-loop mandate.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Principle 7 (Reliability, Safety and Control): human oversight and intervention capability. Principle-level without specific mechanisms.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: GOVERN-1.4 and MANAGE functions address human oversight. Risk-proportionate human involvement recommended.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Principle 4: robustness, security and safety — includes human oversight. Recommends human intervention capability.

singapore-maigf.summary: Human-in-the-loop recommended for high-impact decisions. Agentic AI addendum specifically addresses autonomous agent oversight.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Human oversight embedded in human rights approach. Recommends meaningful human control over AI systems.

eu-ai-act.summary: National competent authorities + AI Office for GPAI. Market surveillance. Fines up to 35M EUR / 7% global turnover. Product bans for non-compliance.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: No enforcement mechanism. Voluntary guidelines with no penalties. Future legislation possible but not currently planned.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: No direct enforcement. Market incentive through adoption. Referenced in some federal procurement requirements.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: Peer review among member countries. Moral authority. Influences national legislation but no direct enforcement.

singapore-maigf.summary: AI Verify testing framework provides soft incentive. PDPC may enforce data protection aspects. No AI-specific penalties.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Readiness Assessment Methodology for self-assessment. No enforcement. Relies on member state implementation.

eu-ai-act.summary: Prohibited AI: 35M EUR / 7% turnover. High-risk non-compliance: 15M EUR / 3%. Incorrect information: 7.5M EUR / 1%. Phased from Feb 2025.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: No penalties. Voluntary guidelines without sanctions or fines.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: No penalties. Voluntary framework. Non-compliance may affect federal contract eligibility.

singapore-maigf.summary: No AI-specific penalties. PDPA penalties may apply for data protection violations in AI systems (up to 10% annual turnover).

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: No penalties. Non-binding recommendation to member states.

eu-ai-act.summary: Aug 2024 (entered into force). Phased: prohibited AI Feb 2025, GPAI Aug 2025, high-risk Aug 2026, full application Aug 2027.

malaysia-ngaige.summary: Sep 2024. Immediately applicable as voluntary guidelines. No phased implementation.

nist-ai-rmf.summary: Jan 2023. Immediately applicable. Companion resources (Generative AI profile, Playbook) published 2024.

oecd-ai-principles.summary: May 2019 (original). Updated May 2024 to address generative AI. Longest-standing international AI principles.

singapore-maigf.summary: Jan 2020 (2nd edition). Agentic AI addendum published 2025. Actively maintained and updated.

unesco-ai-ethics.summary: Nov 2021. First global normative instrument on AI ethics. Readiness assessment published 2023.
